output:
# MIRAGE and CLARION: Curing the Silent, Pooled-Metric-Invisible Comprehension-Failure Gap in Human-Centered XAI with a Comprehension-Conditional, Dissociation-Aware, Exact-Collapse Re-Rendering Layer

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

The research field of eXplainable AI (XAI) has converged on a dominant production pipeline for generating explanations: a trained ML model (neural network, gradient-boosted ensemble, or other opaque predictor) is wrapped by a post-hoc attribution algorithm -- LIME [1], SHAP [4], LRP [3], DeepLIFT [2], Grad-CAM [5], or a counterfactual generator [33] -- that produces a SINGLE explanation artifact (a saliency map, a feature-importance bar chart, a set of contrastive examples, or a natural-language rule) at one FIXED operating point: a frozen choice of content-selection strategy (which features to surface), verbosity (how much detail), modality (visual, textual, hybrid), and abstraction-level (instance-level attribution vs. concept-level summary). The resulting explanation is then rendered to every user in an identical form, irrespective of that user's domain expertise, cognitive style, prior familiarity with the model, or task context. The technical XAI algorithms themselves have reached considerable maturity -- faithfulness metrics, axiom-based comparisons [28], and benchmark suites exist -- but the INTERFACE that delivers the explanation to the human is essentially a static display pipeline: compute attribution, render once, present to all. An influential counter-position argues that for high-stakes decisions one should deploy inherently interpretable models rather than post-hoc explanations of opaque ones [11]; in practice, however, the dominant deployed pattern remains an opaque predictor wrapped by a post-hoc explanation interface, and it is precisely that delivery interface -- not the choice of model -- that this work targets.

Evaluation of these interfaces has followed two largely separate traditions. The HCI/HCXAI community has emphasized SUBJECTIVE measures: user-reported satisfaction, perceived trust, preference rankings, and self-reported understanding, collected via Likert-scale surveys or semi-structured interviews [9, 29]. A smaller but growing line of work uses OBJECTIVE behavioral measures: forward-simulation accuracy (can the user predict the model's output on a new instance given the explanation?), appropriate reliance (does the user follow the model when it is correct and override it when it is wrong?), and task performance (does the explanation improve the user's decision quality?). The landmark finding by Narayanan et al. [8] -- that over 30% of users were unable to understand XAI explanations sufficiently well to use them even in relatively simple tasks -- was established using an objective simulatability protocol, not a satisfaction survey. Crucially, multiple studies have now documented a DISSOCIATION between the two families of measures: users can report high satisfaction and high perceived understanding while demonstrating poor forward-simulation accuracy and exhibiting automation bias [13, 26, 30, 14]. Kaur et al. [26] found that data scientists using interpretability tools expressed strong confidence in their understanding while making systematic errors, an instance of the "illusion of explanatory depth" [31] imported into the XAI context.

The dissociation has a direct implication for pooled evaluation. When the metric used to judge an explanation interface is MEAN satisfaction or MEAN self-reported understanding, averaged over all participants, a system that produces high subjective ratings for the majority while leaving 30% or more objectively uncomprehending will appear to perform well on the dashboard. The comprehension-failure subpopulation -- empirically concentrated among novices, users with low domain-literacy, and users whose cognitive style is mismatched to the explanation modality (e.g., verbal reasoners presented with saliency maps) -- is averaged away, and the signal that the interface is failing them is SILENT under the pooled metric. This is not a hypothetical scenario: it is the direct reading of the Narayanan et al. [8] finding combined with the Kaur et al. [26] dissociation evidence. The field MEASURES the failure (diagnostically, via studies like these), but the deployed interface does not ACT on it: after the study concludes, the same fixed explanation is rendered to every subsequent user in the same way. Miller [6] argued that XAI must import insights from the social sciences of explanation -- selectivity, contrastiveness, social framing -- but the delivery mechanism remains a static, one-shot rendering pipeline that cannot observe whether those insights reached the user.

This diagnostic-but-not-corrective posture defines the opening this work targets. A rich body of HCXAI research now documents WHAT goes wrong (comprehension failure, over-trust, illusion of depth, modality mismatch) and for WHOM (novices, domain-outsiders, cognitive-style-mismatched users), using increasingly rigorous objective protocols (forward-simulation [12], appropriate-reliance tasks [13, 21], cognitive forcing functions [14]). Nguyen and Zhu [24] call for better involvement of human participants in XAI research; Mohseni, Zarei, and Ragan [9] provide a multidisciplinary framework for design and evaluation; Nielsen and Molich [25] established heuristic evaluation as a foundation for interface assessment. But the explanation interface itself remains a static, one-size-fits-all rendering pipeline that is structurally INCAPABLE of responding to this evidence at serving time. The gap between a measured, structured, user-dependent comprehension failure on one side and a user-blind, comprehension-agnostic rendering pipeline on the other -- and the fact that this gap is invisible under the field's preferred pooled subjective metric -- is the opening this work addresses.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap MIRAGE (Metric-Induced Rendering Agnosia under Grouped Evaluation). The deployed HCXAI explanation interface decides the explanation's content-selection, verbosity, modality, and framing at a SINGLE user-blind operating point, structurally incapable of expressing the user's comprehension state. Agnosia -- the inability to recognize or interpret stimuli despite intact sensory function -- is the apt clinical analogy: the interface "sees" the user (renders to them) but cannot recognize whether they understood. The field's dominant evaluation proxy, pooled subjective satisfaction, DISSOCIATES from objective comprehension: a user can report high perceived understanding while failing a forward-simulation task [26] or exhibiting automation bias and over-reliance [13]. Because the pooled metric marginalizes over users, a system whose objective comprehension-failure rate exceeds 30% for a specific subpopulation can still report healthy mean satisfaction, making the failure SILENT on every standard dashboard. MIRAGE names precisely this three-way conjunction: (i) a static, comprehension-agnostic rendering pipeline, (ii) a subjective/objective dissociation in the evaluation proxy, and (iii) a pooled metric that averages away subpopulation-level comprehension failure. The loss SCALES with user-interface mismatch (novice/expert gap, verbal/visual cognitive-style mismatch, low/high domain-literacy) and VANISHES if and only if the fixed explanation is universally comprehensible -- a condition empirically falsified by the greater-than-30% finding of Narayanan et al. [8].

We introduce CLARION (Comprehension-Linked Adaptive Rendering with Identity-collapse On Null-signal), a comprehension-conditional explanation re-rendering controller that closes MIRAGE without retraining the XAI algorithm or the underlying ML model. CLARION sits ON TOP of the deployed attribution output: the XAI algorithm (LIME [1], SHAP [4], LRP [3], Grad-CAM [5], counterfactual generator [33]) runs exactly as before and produces its standard attribution artifact; CLARION then selects a PRESENTATION action from a discrete action space -- content-selection (feature-importance subset, decision rule, counterfactual, prototype-based example), verbosity (concise/standard/detailed), modality (textual/visual/hybrid), and framing (contrastive "why A not B" vs. attributive "because of X") -- conditioned on an OBSERVABLE OBJECTIVE comprehension signal. This signal is derived from two sources: (a) behavioral interaction traces (dwell time on explanation elements, re-inspection sequences, scroll-back patterns) that passively estimate engagement and confusion without interrupting the user, and (b) lightweight forward-simulation probes -- micro-tasks asking the user to predict the model's output on a minimally modified instance -- that directly measure whether the user has built a functional mental model of the explained decision. Critically, CLARION fits SEPARATE policy heads for SUBJECTIVE satisfaction and DEMONSTRATED objective understanding (forward-simulation accuracy, appropriate reliance), because these empirically dissociate. A single-objective controller that maximizes user satisfaction alone risks inflating the illusion of explanatory depth [30, 31] and increasing over-trust (automation bias), exactly the pathology the dissociation literature documents. By maintaining separate targets, CLARION can repair objective comprehension -- re-rendering explanations into more comprehensible forms (e.g., switching from a saliency map to a contrastive example for a verbally-oriented novice) -- while monitoring that subjective satisfaction does not collapse, and vice versa.

The policy parameters are estimated with HIERARCHICAL PARTIAL POOLING [27] across user-cell covariates (domain expertise level, cognitive style proxy, task complexity tier). Partial pooling borrows strength from data-rich cells (e.g., many novice users on standard tasks) into data-poor cells (e.g., few expert users on complex tasks), avoiding the brittleness of fully unpooled per-cell estimates while still permitting cell-specific adaptation where data supports it. This is the statistical counterpart of the presentation-layer design: just as the action space is structured (modality x verbosity x framing x content-selection), the estimation pools across cells at the right granularity. We hypothesize that CLARION will re-render explanations for the greater-than-30% comprehension-failure subpopulation -- selecting a different modality, verbosity level, or framing that better matches their cognitive profile -- while leaving the majority (who already comprehend the static explanation) on the original rendering. The expected outcome is a clinically meaningful reduction in the comprehension-failure rate as measured by forward-simulation accuracy [12] without degrading subjective satisfaction for the majority, and with measurable improvement in appropriate reliance (reduced automation bias and reduced under-reliance).

The decisive design property -- and the deployability unlock -- is the EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee. Under the comprehension-blind identity default (clamping the objective comprehension signal to its prior/uninformative value, as if no interaction traces or forward-simulation probes were observed), CLARION reproduces the deployed STATIC explanation rendering DECISION-FOR-DECISION: every user receives exactly the same explanation they would have received from the unmodified XAI interface, with collapse error E_collapse <= 1e-6 enforced by a CI-gateable build check. This is a strict, safe generalization: zero regression at the identity default, zero regression on pooled satisfaction, and turning the comprehension-conditional machinery on can only ADD corrections the baseline misses. The novelty wedge comprises four clauses that no prior line of HCXAI work covers more than two of simultaneously: C1 -- operates at the presentation/interaction layer on top of the deployed XAI attribution output (no XAI/model retraining); C2 -- conditions on an observable objective comprehension signal (interaction traces plus forward-simulation probes), not self-reported satisfaction; C3 -- models the subjective/objective comprehension dissociation with separate policy heads and targets, repairing demonstrated understanding without inflating over-trust; C4 -- provably exact-collapses to the deployed static explanation at the comprehension-blind identity default (E_collapse <= 1e-6, CI-gateable), guaranteeing zero regression for currently-served users. Adaptive explanation systems [32, 22] cover C1 but not C2-C4; cognitive forcing functions [14] target aspects of C2 and C3 but lack exact-collapse (C4) and do not condition on objective comprehension signals in a closed loop; trust calibration work [17] addresses reliance but not the presentation-layer re-rendering (C1) or the dissociation-aware dual-target design (C3). CLARION is, to our knowledge, the first proposal that fuses all four clauses into a single, formally collapse-safe comprehension-conditional explanation rendering layer.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field (Machine Learning / Human-Centered Explainable AI): technical XAI produces faithful attributions, but the interface that delivers them is a static, user-blind rendering whose comprehension failures are silent under pooled subjective metrics; the open opportunity is a comprehension-conditional, dissociation-aware re-rendering layer that is provably safe to deploy. Here are the five core questions.

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can a comprehension-conditional explanation re-rendering layer that sits on top of a deployed XAI attribution output, conditions presentation actions (content-selection, verbosity, modality, contrastive-vs-attributive framing) on an observable objective comprehension signal (behavioral interaction traces and forward-simulation probes) rather than self-reported satisfaction, fits separate targets for subjective satisfaction and demonstrated understanding to respect their empirical dissociation, and provably exact-collapses to the static baseline at the comprehension-blind default (E_collapse <= 1e-6), close the greater-than-30% comprehension-failure gap that is currently silent under pooled evaluation metrics in human-centered explainable AI interfaces?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 - A broad latent vulnerability in the dominant HCXAI design pattern. HCXAI is deployed across high-stakes human-AI decision-making domains -- healthcare diagnostics, financial credit scoring, criminal justice risk assessment, and public-sector resource allocation [9, 18, 19, 20]. In every deployment, the dominant design pattern is a SINGLE static explanation rendered identically to every user: one fixed content-selection, one verbosity level, one modality, one abstraction level [6, 7]. This means any structural blind spot in that fixed rendering is not an edge case but a BROAD latent failure propagated to every user and every decision the system touches. MIRAGE names exactly such a blind spot: the deployed XAI interface operates at a user-BLIND operating point, structurally incapable of detecting whether THIS user COMPREHENDED the explanation it rendered. When deployed at scale in healthcare triage or credit adjudication, the consequence is systematic under-informing of a user subpopulation across every decision they make.

P2 - The failure is silent and metric-invisible. The field evaluates XAI systems primarily against POOLED SUBJECTIVE proxies -- mean satisfaction scores, self-reported understanding, Likert-scale preference [8, 15] -- that DISSOCIATE from OBJECTIVE comprehension measures such as forward-simulation accuracy and appropriate reliance [12, 15, 16]. Narayanan et al. [8] found that over 30% of users could not understand XAI explanations sufficiently well to use them even in simple tasks, yet this failure is invisible under pooled mean metrics that average over users. The field is "optimizing the wrong target": a system that maximizes mean user satisfaction may achieve high aggregate scores while systematically failing the most vulnerable subpopulation. Naming this dissociation between subjective proxy and objective comprehension, MEASURING it with stratified comprehension metrics rather than pooled averages, and diagnosing which user subgroups are invisible under current evaluation practices is itself a substantive contribution to the HCXAI evaluation literature.

P3 - Fairness and equity along a comprehension axis. The users harmed by MIRAGE are not randomly distributed. They are disproportionately novices, users with low domain literacy, and individuals with mismatched cognitive styles relative to the single explanation modality offered [6, 7, 8, 20, 24]. These are often the most vulnerable stakeholders in high-stakes AI decision systems -- patients navigating diagnostic explanations, applicants reviewing credit decisions, citizens interacting with public-sector AI [9, 18]. Disparate comprehension outcomes across user groups under a flat aggregate metric is a textbook equity problem: the system appears to "work" on average while systematically disadvantaging a specific subpopulation. CLARION's comprehension-stratified evaluation and per-cell repair mechanism reframes HCXAI usability as a measurable equity obligation, not merely a design preference.

P4 - Deployability with provable zero regression. CLARION is designed as an adoptable COMPONENT, not a research-only analysis. It operates entirely at the presentation/interaction layer on top of deployed XAI attribution output (LIME [1], SHAP [4], LRP [3], Grad-CAM [5], counterfactual [33]) -- no retraining of the XAI algorithm or the underlying ML model is required (clause C1). Its EXACT-COLLAPSE property guarantees that when the comprehension signal is clamped to its uninformative prior default, CLARION reproduces the deployed static explanation decision-for-decision (E_collapse <= 1e-6, CI-gateable) (clause C4). This enables a ship-in-shadow deployment: CLARION runs alongside the existing system with provably zero regression for currently-served users, then is dialed up only for user cells where comprehension monitoring detects failure. The safety guarantee lowers the adoption barrier from "replace your XAI pipeline" to "add a presentation adapter with an off-switch."

P5 - Generality: a comprehension-conditional design-and-evaluation recipe. The comprehension-conditional adaptation pattern with dissociation-aware dual targets (demonstrated understanding + calibrated reliance, separately from satisfaction) and exact-collapse safety is a GENERAL HCXAI design-and-evaluation recipe, not specific to one XAI algorithm or decision domain. It operationalizes the core diagnosis articulated by the survey [9] and the human-centered XAI agenda [6, 7, 18, 19, 20]: that technical XAI methods produce explanations that lack usability for real users, and that bridging the gap requires closing the loop between explanation rendering and human comprehension. CLARION's architecture applies wherever a deployed explanation system renders static output to heterogeneous users -- medical imaging, natural language rationales, tabular feature attributions, counterfactual examples -- making the comprehension-conditional presentation layer a transferable contribution that bridges the technical-XAI / HCI divide the Introduction highlights.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 (BINDING) - DISSOCIATION DEFEATS A NAIVE SATISFACTION OBJECTIVE. Subjective satisfaction and self-reported understanding DISSOCIATE from objective comprehension (forward-simulation accuracy, appropriate reliance). Kaur et al. [26] showed data scientists over-trusted SHAP/LIME outputs they could not actually use; Bucinca et al. [14] demonstrated that cognitive-forcing interventions that lowered satisfaction raised appropriate reliance. The dissociation is structural: explanations that feel fluent and complete trigger an illusion of explanatory depth [31], inflating confidence without improving demonstrated understanding. A naive controller that optimizes a single "make users happier / more confident" objective is REPRESENTATIONALLY incapable of jointly repairing demonstrated understanding AND calibrating appropriate reliance -- it will hill-climb toward persuasion, increasing over-trust and automation bias [13] while objective comprehension stays flat or drops. This forces SEPARATE subjective and objective reward heads (exactly C3): the objective head targets forward-simulation accuracy and appropriate reliance; the subjective head tracks satisfaction as a secondary signal to avoid gratuitous UX degradation; the two are never collapsed into one scalar.

O2 - LATENT, PER-USER COMPREHENSION AND DATA SPARSITY. Comprehension is a latent construct that varies across users and contexts. The cells that most need repair -- novices, low-domain-literacy users, users with mismatched cognitive styles -- are exactly the cells where data is sparsest, because they are under-represented in study populations and produce fewer interactions before disengaging. Per-user random effects (prior knowledge, reading speed, visualization literacy) are large relative to the conditional signal from any single explanation rendering. A naive per-cell maximum-likelihood fit is unidentifiable or overfits in these critical cells. This is a REPRESENTATIONAL problem, not merely a sample-size problem: even with more data, a fixed-effects-only model cannot express the partial-information regime where a user has seen 3-5 explanations. The solution requires HIERARCHICAL / PARTIAL POOLING [27]: shrink data-poor cells toward the population prior while allowing data-rich cells to deviate, and propagate uncertainty into the policy so that under-observed cells receive the safe identity-default rendering rather than a high-variance adaptive guess.

O3 - CONFOUNDED OBSERVABLE SIGNAL. The interaction traces that a comprehension-state estimator must consume -- dwell time, re-reads, hovers, feature-expansion clicks, scroll depth -- conflate ENGAGED-understanding with CONFUSED-struggling. A user who dwells 12 seconds on an explanation may be deeply processing it or may be lost. These traces also correlate with task difficulty, UI layout, and individual reading speed, creating confounders that a naive trace-driven controller will mis-attribute: it will interpret confusion-driven long dwell as engagement and serve more complex explanations, worsening the failure. This is a REPRESENTATIONAL problem because the trace space itself is ambiguous without an external anchor. The solution requires (a) WITHIN-USER contrasts (change in trace pattern relative to the same user's baseline, not absolute values) and (b) lightweight forward-SIMULATION probes (objective ground truth: "what would the model predict if feature X changed?") embedded as micro-interactions, which provide a deconfounded comprehension signal that the traces alone cannot.

O4 - ADAPTIVITY BACKFIRE, FAIRNESS, AND MANIPULATION. Conditioning the explanation rendering on user covariates (expertise level, domain literacy, demographic group) can create DISPARATE explanation quality across subpopulations -- some groups systematically receive simpler or less informative explanations, even if this improves their local satisfaction score. Worse, an adapt-to-satisfaction loop can become a DARK PATTERN: the system learns that vague, reassuring explanations maximize user-reported understanding while minimizing actual scrutiny, effectively PERSUADING rather than INFORMING. This is a REPRESENTATIONAL problem because an aggregate-loss objective (mean satisfaction or mean task accuracy) cannot express the constraint "no group should receive systematically worse comprehension outcomes." The solution requires (a) an explicit appropriate-reliance objective (users should accept correct AI advice and reject incorrect AI advice, not blindly trust) [21] alongside an equal-comprehension-across-cells constraint, and (b) the identity-default safety net from C4: any cell where the adaptive policy's effect is uncertain reverts to the static baseline, preventing speculative adaptation from degrading currently-served experience.

O5 - METRIC BLINDNESS. Standard HCXAI evaluation reports POOLED MEAN satisfaction and POOLED MEAN task accuracy. These aggregated metrics structurally CANNOT detect (a) the subpopulation comprehension failure (the >30% who cannot use explanations are averaged away), (b) the dissociation between subjective and objective understanding (both are collapsed into one number), or (c) over-trust (high task accuracy when AI is correct + low accuracy when AI is incorrect looks fine in the average but indicates automation bias). Progress on CLARION is UNMEASURABLE under standard metrics. This is a REPRESENTATIONAL problem: the metric space lacks the dimensionality to see the phenomenon. The solution requires COMPREHENSION-STRATIFIED, DISSOCIATION-AWARE metrics as first-class outputs: per-cell forward-simulation accuracy, per-cell over-reliance rate (accept-incorrect), per-cell under-reliance rate (reject-correct), trust calibration (AUC of user reliance vs AI correctness), and the subjective-objective gap (confidence minus forward-simulation accuracy), all reported as DISAGGREGATED per-cell tables alongside (but never replaced by) the pooled aggregate.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

CLARION's novelty is the JOINT conjunction of four design clauses -- C1 (presentation-layer adaptation, no XAI/model retraining), C2 (conditioning on observable objective comprehension signals, not static user models or self-reported satisfaction), C3 (dissociation-aware dual-target calibration separating demonstrated understanding from subjective satisfaction to repair comprehension without inflating over-trust), and C4 (provable exact-collapse to the deployed static explanation at the comprehension-blind default, providing zero-regression safety). No prior line of work in HCXAI covers more than two of these four clauses simultaneously. Below, we position CLARION against the five closest near-miss families, identifying which clauses each covers and which it structurally misses.

NEAR-MISS 1: User-Adaptive / Personalized Explanations. Conati et al. [22] and related work on expertise-based or preference-based explanation selection [18, 20] adapt explanation content or format to user characteristics. These approaches COVER C1 (presentation-layer adaptation) and partially C2 (they condition on user features). However, they condition on STATIC user models -- demographics, expertise level, self-reported preferences -- not on an OBSERVABLE OBJECTIVE comprehension signal measured in real time from interaction traces and forward-simulation probes (MISSES full C2). They optimize a single objective (typically user satisfaction or preference) and do not model the dissociation between subjective satisfaction and demonstrated understanding with separate targets (MISSES C3). They provide no formal exact-collapse guarantee to the deployed static explanation under a comprehension-blind default (MISSES C4). Covers: C1 + partial C2. Misses: full C2, C3, C4.

NEAR-MISS 2: Interactive / Conversational XAI. Slack et al. [23] demonstrate TalkToModel, enabling users to query models conversationally through natural language dialogue, and Lakkaraju et al. [38] argue for rethinking explainability as a dialogue. This approach COVERS C1 (presentation-layer interaction, no model retraining). However, the dialogue is driven by USER-INITIATED queries, not by a system that conditions explanation re-rendering on an observable objective comprehension signal -- the system does not monitor whether the user actually understood (MISSES C2). The engagement objective is singular (user engagement, question-answering accuracy, or satisfaction) with no dual-target decomposition separating demonstrated comprehension from subjective satisfaction (MISSES C3). No exact-collapse guarantee to the static baseline is provided or discussed (MISSES C4). Covers: C1. Misses: C2, C3, C4.

NEAR-MISS 3: Mental-Model / Simulatability Evaluation. Hase and Bansal [12] and Doshi-Velez and Kim [10] develop forward-simulation tasks and evaluation frameworks that MEASURE whether users can predict model behavior given explanations. Bucinca et al. [15] demonstrate that proxy tasks and subjective measures can be misleading in evaluating XAI systems. This body of work grounds the C2/C3 axis as DIAGNOSIS: it demonstrates that forward-simulation accuracy is a meaningful measure of objective comprehension and that it can dissociate from subjective understanding reports [12, 15, 16]. However, this line is EVALUATION-ONLY: it measures comprehension but does not build a closed-loop repair controller that conditions explanation re-rendering on the measured signal (MISSES C1-as-controller). It provides diagnostic evidence but no adaptive method. No exact-collapse guarantee is defined because there is no adaptive system to collapse (MISSES C4). Covers: partial C2 + partial C3 (as measurement only). Misses: C1-as-controller, C4.

NEAR-MISS 4: Appropriate-Reliance / Trust-Calibration Interventions. Bansal et al. [13] study how explanations affect complementary team performance, Bucinca et al. [14] introduce cognitive forcing functions to reduce over-reliance, and Vasconcelos et al. [21] show that explanation design can reduce overreliance on AI systems during decision-making. Zhang et al. [17] investigate the joint effect of confidence and explanation on trust calibration. These approaches target the right outcome (appropriate reliance, trust calibration) and partially address C3 by distinguishing appropriate from inappropriate trust. However, they deploy FIXED interventions (e.g., always show cognitive forcing, always withhold explanations in certain conditions) rather than a comprehension-CONDITIONAL rendering controller that adapts to an observable comprehension signal (MISSES C2 + C1-as-adaptive-controller). They do not separate subjective satisfaction from demonstrated understanding as dual targets inside an adaptive controller (partial C3 at best -- they diagnose the distinction but do not operationalize it as separate optimization targets). No exact-collapse safety guarantee to the deployed static baseline is provided (MISSES C4). Covers: C1 (fixed presentation change) + partial C3 (diagnoses reliance). Misses: C2, full C3-as-method, C4.

NEAR-MISS 5: Interpretability-as-Rigorous-Science / Measuring Interpretability. Poursabzi-Sangdeh et al. [16] systematically manipulate model interpretability factors and measure their effects on user behavior, and Kaur et al. [26] reveal that data scientists over-trust and misuse interpretability tools even when the tools are technically correct. This work provides rigorous MEASUREMENT of how explanation properties affect user comprehension and reliance -- grounding the importance of objective evaluation. However, like Near-Miss 3, it is diagnostic/evaluative: it does not build a conditional repair controller (MISSES C1-as-controller). It measures but does not adapt. No exact-collapse guarantee is defined (MISSES C4). The dual-target structure is implicit in findings (users report confidence but demonstrate poor simulation accuracy) but is not operationalized as separate optimization targets within a controller (MISSES C3-as-method). Covers: partial C2 + partial C3 (as empirical evidence). Misses: C1-as-controller, full C3-as-method, C4.

COVERAGE GAPS. Across these five near-miss families, four specific capabilities remain unclaimed in the HCXAI literature: (a) No CLOSED-LOOP COMPREHENSION-CONDITIONAL explanation controller exists that conditions explanation re-rendering on an OBSERVABLE OBJECTIVE comprehension signal (interaction traces + forward-simulation probes) rather than static user models, self-reported preferences, or user-initiated queries. (b) No DISSOCIATION-AWARE DUAL-TARGET calibration has been implemented inside an adaptive controller that explicitly separates demonstrated understanding from subjective satisfaction as distinct optimization targets, repairing objective comprehension without inflating over-trust. (c) No EXACT-COLLAPSE / SAFE-GENERALIZATION guarantee has been defined for adaptive HCXAI systems: a formal property ensuring that the adaptive system reproduces the deployed static explanation decision-for-decision when the comprehension signal is clamped to its uninformative default, providing zero-regression safety and enabling ship-in-shadow deployment. (d) No COMPREHENSION-STRATIFIED EVALUATION has been adopted as a first-class metric in HCXAI: evaluating explanation effectiveness per user cell (stratified by comprehension outcome, cognitive style, domain expertise) rather than pooled across users, making the >30% failure subpopulation visible rather than averaged away. CLARION is the sole occupant of the C1 AND C2 AND C3 AND C4 conjunction, combining presentation-layer comprehension-conditional adaptation, observable objective comprehension signals, dissociation-aware dual targets, and exact-collapse safety into a single deployable system.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Q5a - METHOD. Base setup: a deployed binary classifier on a high-stakes decision task with a standard post-hoc XAI method producing a fixed attribution explanation. For tabular tasks: a gradient-boosted tree or logistic regression on FICO HELOC (Explainable ML Challenge) or COMPAS recidivism data, explained by SHAP [4] or LIME [1]. For image tasks: a ResNet or DenseNet on CheXpert chest X-rays [35], explained by Grad-CAM [5] or counterfactual visual explanations [33]. The XAI method and base model are FROZEN; CLARION operates entirely in the presentation layer. CLARION controller: a contextual policy over a discrete/structured ACTION SPACE with the following dimensions: (1) content selection (which top-k features/regions to highlight; k in {3, 5, 7, all}); (2) verbosity level (terse / standard / detailed); (3) modality (text-only, visual-only, text+visual); (4) explanation frame (attribution/importance vs contrastive/counterfactual vs example-based nearest-neighbor); (5) abstraction level (raw feature values vs natural-language summaries vs analogies). The action space is combinatorial but pruned to ~50-100 feasible rendering configurations per task domain. Comprehension STATE estimation: online, from two signal sources. (a) Interaction traces: dwell time per explanation element, re-read count, hover/expansion events, scroll depth, click-through to details -- all computed as WITHIN-USER z-scores (relative to that user's running baseline) to deconfound individual reading speed and task difficulty. (b) Forward-simulation PROBES: lightweight micro-interactions embedded every 3-5 decisions, asking the user to predict the model's output for a slightly perturbed input ("if this applicant's income increased by $10k, would the model's prediction change?") or to identify the most important feature. Probe accuracy provides an objective ground-truth comprehension signal. Estimator: a HIERARCHICAL BAYESIAN / MIXED-EFFECTS model [27]. User-level random intercepts and slopes capture individual comprehension trajectories. Covariates include user expertise (self-reported + probe-measured), domain literacy, visualization literacy (measured via VLAT [34]), and task difficulty. PARTIAL POOLING across user cells: data-poor cells shrink toward the population mean; data-rich cells are free to deviate. The estimator maintains SEPARATE reward heads: (i) OBJECTIVE: forward-simulation probe accuracy + appropriate reliance (accept-correct, reject-incorrect); (ii) SUBJECTIVE: self-reported satisfaction and perceived understanding. These are never combined into a single scalar -- the policy optimizes the objective head subject to a non-degradation constraint on the subjective head. EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee (C4): define the identity-default action as the deployed static rendering configuration. When the comprehension state estimate equals its prior (uninformative default -- equivalent to having observed no user-specific signal), the policy deterministically selects the identity-default action. Implementation: the policy's action-selection function includes a gating term that interpolates between the adaptive action and the identity-default, weighted by the posterior confidence that the comprehension state has deviated from the prior. At the prior, the weight is exactly 1.0 on the identity-default. Verification: E_collapse = max over all test instances of |output(CLARION, state=prior) - output(STATIC)| is enforced <= 1e-6 via a CI gate (automated test that fails the build if violated) (measured on the continuous gating weight that determines discrete action selection; the 1e-6 threshold accommodates floating-point rounding).

Q5b - DATASETS AND STUDY DESIGN. This is a HUMAN-SUBJECTS study (HCI tradition). Decision tasks and datasets: (Tabular) T1 FICO HELOC Explainable ML Challenge (credit risk, ~10k instances, 23 features); T2 COMPAS recidivism (ProPublica version, ~7k instances); T3 German Credit (UCI, 1000 instances, 20 features); T4 UCI Adult Income (48842 instances, 14 features); XAI: SHAP [4] + LIME [1]. (Image) I1 CheXpert chest X-ray classification [35] (multi-label, 224k images; use a binary pneumothorax detection subset); optionally I2 a dermoscopy task (ISIC 2019) or a simpler Quick-Draw sketch classification for broader participant accessibility; XAI: Grad-CAM [5] + counterfactual [33]. Participants: recruited via Prolific (preferred for data quality [36]) with stratified sampling to OVER-SAMPLE the critical cells: low-domain-literacy, novice, low-visualization-literacy (measured by a VLAT pre-screen [34]). Target N = 200-300 per study arm (power analysis for detecting a 10-point difference in forward-simulation accuracy at alpha=0.05, power=0.80, accounting for the hierarchical structure). Design: MIXED within-subjects + between-subjects. Within-subjects: each participant experiences BOTH the static baseline (B1) and CLARION (B5) in counterbalanced order (to control for learning effects). Between-subjects: participants are randomly assigned to one of the baselines B2-B4 in addition to the within-subjects comparison, to keep session length manageable (~45-60 minutes). Held-out USERS: 20% of participants are reserved for testing generalization to unseen user profiles. Held-out COMPREHENSION-CELL extrapolation: train the hierarchical model excluding one expertise-by-domain-literacy cell, then evaluate whether partial pooling recovers reasonable predictions for that cell. Pre-registration on OSF or AsPredicted before data collection. The study protocol will undergo institutional ethics / IRB review prior to participant recruitment, with informed consent and the right to withdraw.

Q5c - METRICS. Primary (objective): Forward-simulation accuracy [12] -- proportion of probes where the user correctly predicts the model's output or counterfactual behavior, STRATIFIED by comprehension cell (expertise x domain-literacy x visualization-literacy); Appropriate reliance -- (a) Accept-correct rate = P(user follows AI advice | AI is correct); (b) Reject-incorrect rate = P(user overrides AI advice | AI is incorrect), reported separately, never averaged; Over-reliance rate = P(user follows AI advice | AI is incorrect); Under-reliance rate = P(user overrides AI advice | AI is correct); Trust calibration -- AUC of the curve plotting user reliance rate against AI correctness rate across binned AI-confidence levels (ideal = diagonal; over-trust = above diagonal). Secondary (subjective): Subjective satisfaction -- Likert-scale ratings of explanation helpfulness, clarity, and trustworthiness (post-task questionnaire; adapted from the Explanation Satisfaction Scale [37]); Subjective-objective GAP -- |user confidence in their understanding - forward-simulation accuracy|, per cell (a shrinking gap indicates better calibration); Time-to-comprehension -- seconds from explanation presentation to the user's first correct action or probe response. Safety: E_collapse = max |output(CLARION, state=prior) - output(STATIC)| <= 1e-6, enforced as a CI gate. Fairness: MAX comprehension gap across cells = max_cell(forward-sim accuracy) - min_cell(forward-sim accuracy), reporting the FULL disaggregated per-cell table, not just the scalar; MAX reliance gap across cells, analogous for appropriate reliance.

Q5d - BASELINES AND ABLATIONS. Baselines: B1 STATIC one-size-fits-all explanation (the deployed default; the exact-collapse target) -- fixed SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM rendering with default verbosity and modality; B2 SATISFACTION-OPTIMIZING adaptive controller -- same action space as CLARION but optimizes ONLY subjective satisfaction (single reward head), predicted to inflate over-trust, existing specifically to FALSIFY O1 by demonstrating empirically that satisfaction optimization dissociates from objective understanding; B3 DEMOGRAPHIC/EXPERTISE personalization only -- adapts rendering based on user expertise and domain-literacy covariates (measured pre-task) but uses NO objective comprehension signal and NO forward-simulation probes [22]; B4 INTERACTIVE/CONVERSATIONAL XAI -- users can ask follow-up questions about the explanation (a menu of pre-structured queries: "why not the alternative?", "show me a similar case", "simplify this explanation"), comparable to TalkToModel [23] or dialogue-based XAI [38]; B5 CLARION (full) -- dual objective heads, hierarchical partial pooling, forward-simulation probes, identity-default exact-collapse. Ablations (all relative to B5): A1 Subjective-only target (collapse to single satisfaction head -- expected to degrade toward B2); A2 Objective-only target (drop subjective head -- expected to sometimes degrade UX without improving comprehension); A3 DUAL target (full B5 -- expected to dominate A1 and A2); A4 Pooled (no partial pooling -- population-level fixed effects only; expected to fail in data-poor cells); A5 PARTIAL-POOLING (full B5 -- expected to dominate A4 especially in novice/low-literacy cells); A6 Without forward-simulation probes (interaction traces only -- expected to suffer from O3 confounding); A7 With forward-simulation probes (full B5 -- expected to dominate A6); A8 Identity-default OFF (adaptive policy applied everywhere, no safety net -- expected to show fairness/backfire issues per O4); A9 Identity-default ON (full B5); A10 Per-modality ablation (restrict action space to text-only, visual-only, or text+visual -- tests which modality dimension contributes most).

Q5e - VALIDATION. Three layers of validation. (1) EXACT-COLLAPSE CI GATE: an automated test computes E_collapse = max over a suite of >= 10,000 synthetic user-state vectors set to the prior of |CLARION_output - STATIC_output|; the CI pipeline fails if E_collapse > 1e-6, run on every code commit. This test is NECESSARY (not sufficient) for correctness: it guarantees zero regression for currently-served users when no comprehension signal is available. (2) THREE FALSIFIABILITY TRIPLES: F1 (Collapse identity) -- HYPOTHESIS: when the comprehension state is clamped to its prior, CLARION reproduces the static baseline rendering decision-for-decision; FALSIFIED IF E_collapse > 1e-6 on any test instance; validates C4. F2 (Comprehension repair) -- HYPOTHESIS: per-cell forward-simulation accuracy and appropriate reliance in the worst-performing cells (novice/low-literacy) improve under CLARION (B5) vs the static baseline (B1), with the improvement tapering toward zero as the comprehension state approaches the prior; FALSIFIED IF B5 does not statistically significantly outperform B1 on forward-simulation accuracy in the bottom-quartile comprehension cells (one-sided test, alpha=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected); validates C1+C2. F3 (Dissociation demonstration) -- HYPOTHESIS: the satisfaction-only controller (B2) raises subjective satisfaction relative to B1 but does NOT raise forward-simulation accuracy and INCREASES over-reliance; FALSIFIED IF B2 improves both satisfaction AND objective comprehension without increasing over-reliance; validates C3. (3) ROBUSTNESS CHECKS: (a) held-out user generalization (train on 80% of participants, evaluate on 20%); (b) held-out comprehension-cell extrapolation; (c) order effects in the within-subjects design (test for carry-over with counterbalancing); (d) sensitivity analysis on the probe frequency (every 3 vs 5 vs 7 decisions).

Q5f - EXPECTED OUTCOMES (as HYPOTHESES, with ranges). All stated as hypotheses to be tested, not established results. H1: E_collapse <= 1e-6 BY CONSTRUCTION (deterministic, verified by CI gate). H2: Objective forward-simulation accuracy in the worst comprehension cells (bottom quartile by expertise x domain-literacy) is hypothesized to improve by approximately +10 to +25 percentage points under CLARION (B5) relative to the static baseline (B1), tapering monotonically toward zero as the comprehension state approaches the prior (consistent with exact-collapse). H3: Over-reliance rate in the worst cells is hypothesized to decrease by approximately 8-15 percentage points under CLARION (B5) vs B1, reflecting improved appropriate-reliance calibration. H4: Pooled subjective satisfaction under CLARION (B5) is hypothesized to be roughly unchanged or modestly improved (+0 to +5 points on a 100-point scale) relative to B1, while the SUBJECTIVE-OBJECTIVE GAP (|confidence - forward-sim accuracy|) shrinks by approximately 5-15 percentage points. H5: The satisfaction-only controller (B2) is hypothesized to raise satisfaction by +5 to +15 points relative to B1 but INCREASE over-reliance by +5 to +12 percentage points and show NO statistically significant improvement in forward-simulation accuracy -- empirically demonstrating the dissociation (O1/C3). H6: The max across-cell comprehension gap (max_cell - min_cell forward-sim accuracy) is hypothesized to decrease by approximately 10-20 percentage points under CLARION (B5) vs B1, indicating more equitable comprehension outcomes. H7: Demographic/expertise personalization only (B3) is hypothesized to capture approximately 30-50% of CLARION's objective improvement, demonstrating that static covariates help but the online objective comprehension signal provides substantial additional value. H8: The partial-pooling ablation (A5) is hypothesized to outperform the pooled ablation (A4) specifically in the data-poor novice/low-literacy cells, with diminishing advantage in data-rich cells.

Q5g - Responsible-AI and deployment considerations. CLARION conditions on comprehension signals and (optionally) user covariates, which raises privacy, fairness, and manipulation risks that the design explicitly manages. (i) Data minimization and privacy: interaction traces and probe responses are processed as within-user contrasts and aggregated comprehension-cell statistics; CLARION does not require demographic attributes at inference and can run in a covariate-light mode that conditions on the online objective signal alone. Behavioral traces and probe responses are governed by a stated retention-and-deletion schedule; dataset licenses are verified for secondary research use before IRB submission (COMPAS is sensitive criminal-justice data; CheXpert carries a research-only license); the adaptive nature of the interface is disclosed in participant consent with a post-study debrief; and the Prolific data-processing agreement is confirmed to cover behavioral-trace collection. (ii) Anti-manipulation and trust calibration: because the objective head targets demonstrated understanding and appropriate reliance (not satisfaction), and because the satisfaction-only baseline B2 is included precisely to detect persuasion/dark-pattern behavior, the evaluation actively surfaces any drift toward reassuring-but-uninformative explanations; the calibration target is BIDIRECTIONAL (guarding against both over-reliance and under-reliance), not merely over-trust reduction. (iii) Fairness: outcomes are reported as a full disaggregated per-(expertise x domain-literacy x cognitive-style) table for forward-simulation accuracy and appropriate reliance, not only a scalar max-gap, and an equal-comprehension-across-cells constraint is part of the objective. Because expertise and literacy covariates can proxy protected attributes (socioeconomic status, education, race), available demographic breakdowns are included in the disaggregated table, and for the COMPAS domain comprehension-improvement is reported by participant demographics to verify that adaptation corrects rather than amplifies the base model's known racial disparities. (iv) High-stakes safeguards: in healthcare, credit, and justice settings CLARION informs but must not be the sole arbiter of the human decision; the explanation interface should preserve user agency and provide an accessible path to request an alternative explanation or human review. (v) Safe rollout and auditability: the exact-collapse identity default makes ship-in-shadow deployment auditable -- any cell with uncertain adaptive benefit reverts to the currently-served static explanation, so the system can only add repair, never silently degrade; deployment additionally logs, per decision, the chosen rendering action, the comprehension-state estimate, and the identity-default flag for post-hoc audit, and a runtime circuit-breaker triggers automatic identity-default reversion if the live over-reliance rate exceeds the static baseline by more than 5 percentage points.
